speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five, [? George ?] Kohn, February 23, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very anxious to-- to know when I should establish a yahrzeit after my parents. So what happened? I imagined, and I found out from different sources, that my parents went to Treblinka. And that-- that date was-- would be roughly around November-- November the 10th-- no, 10 days-- a Jewish calendar is established a date because it must be around November the-- November the 10th, which-- 1942. And that's what happened. So that's-- on-- on this basis, I established my yahrzeit, which I observe every year, and I go to shul, say Kaddish and everything, and that's the way it-- it happened. And after that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your business--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and also, we lost at the same time, I forgot to mention before, we had an uncle by the name Rosenberg. You know. He lived right beside us, and this whole family was wiped out. He also went to the same train. You know. And that was the-- the story. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your business.

speaker: interviewee
text: My business. My business was very successful. I had-- I had one place, one building, almost 32 years at the same building. In 1969, one of my customers approached me. We should go in the retail business unless the political situation in Montreal has changed a little bit with the separatists, and they wanted to separate. And he told me it's [? gonna be ?] dangerous to go into a different state, to Ontario.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, we went into Ontario. We opened up a store. From one store became six. And being in the manufacturing business, I had access to all the mills and all. It was in the textile business. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very successful. Well, my partner passed away, and I had to pay out the widow. We had a business agreement. And I had those stores maybe for five years. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the name of the-- the name of the stores was [? Manufacturer's ?] Mart. You know. And it was like from the-- from the factory to the consumer. Like, it was a gimmick-- a business gimmick. So it wasn't-- but something happened to that business, you know. One day, I was-- I used to go to every weekend to check that business, being in the manufacturing business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, I found out-- they called-- I wasn't home-- they called my wife that there was a fire. And the warehouse, everything burned down. And I had insurance for $100,000, but the insurance never wanted to pay me. So I figured it was the time to give it up, and I gave the bank the policy. You collect the money, and that's it. That was the end of the business. But after-- before that, I gave my children the manufacturing business. I gave them the key. I didn't mention-- [INAUDIBLE] , tell me about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave to my sons the business, but they didn't want to keep it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stanley Howard, Stanley and Jeffrey. But they were too young to operate, and I had no time. I was involved with the retail business, so I had no time to stay with them. Otherwise, they would have survived, but they moved out of-- of the province. They moved to-- to Toronto. And I didn't want to go back to business anymore. So I had a home in Montreal. I had a-- not the original home when I bought. That was the third home. We bought a beautiful home in the suburb, and it was a question.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in 1973, we bought this place in Deerfield, which I didn't intend to have. I just bought it for speculation in case we decided to come to Florida. And then we bought a place in 1957. We bought a place in the country, you know, in the mountains-- these large mountains. We used to go there for weekends, and I used to go skiing in the winter, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I lost-- I gave up all my business, we-- we had to make a decision, what to-- what to do with our life. You know. So we-- we decided not to keep three homes. It was too much. The home in the city was too big. It was eight rooms, and the kids were married. They all-- both got married. You know. I'll tell you later when they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, who did they marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- so we decided to sell the house in the city, and we're gonna stay in the mountains, let's say, six months. And we're going to stay here six months, and it worked. In the meantime, it was only 22 years and everything worked out nicely enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are from Quebec altogether, which we sold the house in the-- in the country too. And we're going to move. We bought a place not far from the kids on the lake, you know, to be on the lake. And  we're going to take possession in-- in March. Tell me about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my children are-- 19-- 1952, my first son was born, named Stanley. And he was born-- he was married to-- he was born in '52. And then next, in 1954 my second son was born, second son, Jeffrey, was born in '54. And Stanley got married in 19-- oh, I forgot the number.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: And he has children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stanley was born in '75.

speaker: interviewee
text: '75, '75 was born to Marlene, and Jeffrey was married in '77 right here in Deerfield in our synagogue. He was-- he married Nina Moskowic. And Stanley has two-- a daughter, which she was bat mitzvahed last year. She had a wonderful little affair.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amanda, and she's going to be 14 now. And their boy, Ryan, is 11 years old, which he's going to be bar mitzvahed soon. And they're very happy and very successful. They've got a good business, and she has a good job. She works as a-- a public relation-- marketing. And she's doing very well, my daughter-in-law. And Jeffrey's doing all right. He's doing very good. Now they want us to move down to their place to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's nice. Tell me-- I have one question for you about Wenzel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the war with Wenzel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, sure, I didn't get it. I forgot all about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You haven't mentioned Jeff's family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jeff's family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and my-- my son-- the other son, Jeff, has three kids. The older one is Gina. She's-- she's 13, gonna be 13 in May. And Marley is--

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 years old, and the youngest boy, Joshua, is three and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, nice family.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are wonderful children. We love them all. We enjoy them very much. What I meant to say--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wenzel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, at 19-- while I was in business, I decided to do something for a best friend, which he was responsible for saving our life, well, most of it. We decided-- I-- I made a promise myself. If I ever have a chance, I have to see this man again and bring him down-- bring him to Canada. [? I know ?] [? this ?] [? year's ?] 1971, business was very good. I could afford it, so I sent him a ticket to come to visit me. He was very happy. He arrived. And naturally, they made you find out-- a few local newspapers found out the story. And they all came down to receive him, and they showed him pictures and stories, asking questions. He was very happy to see me, you know. And he stayed in my house for six weeks, you know, dining and wining. And he used to come to the factory. He used to ship parcels every day to the family. You know. Like, I was in the sportswear business, whatever he could.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a friend of mine, I took him to meet my friends. So a friend of mine greeted him, and he put a hundred dollars in his hand. And he asked him, what's that for? I said to him, there's one-- one Gentile. He could help one Jew. He's worth everything. The same friend of mine, when Wenzel left, went back to Poland. So he sent him maybe-- must be how long? '71, maybe 25 years. He put in an envelope every month, the first every month, $20, which was worth in Poland six months of living, you know, for that money to change in their exchange. So it was a wonderful thing this man did. His name was Alvin [? Steinwald. ?] He's alive. He's in [? Hollywood ?] here. And right away, three years ago, I had   a notice that Mr. Wenzel passed away. And now the widow is still alive, so I keep touch. And I send  her for Christmas, I put some money in an envelope. And that was the end of the story of Wenzel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Israel found out. Yad Vashem found out about this story from the-- from the newspaper probably, and they decided to plant a tree in his name, which I was in Israel three years ago. And I checked it, and I found his name there. So I thought he was a wonderful person. He's worth to talk about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to tell your story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, why I decided to tell the story? I was-- for the last 50 years, I couldn't wait to tell the whole story. Now, people know my story, bits and pieces, you know. But like you're doing now, I-- I would not have a chance. I didn't know whom to tell the story to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Because it was very important. This is very important. Important-- it is important to my children, which they always bug me. Tell me. Tell me, and tell me. And I couldn't tell him. This would be a great thing, which I'm grateful to you people anyway. So now they've got the whole story. It's for my children, for my great-- grandchildren, great grandchildren. And let them know what happened. What message would you like to leave for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: For future generations? Don't forget. Never forget. And keep the memory alive, and-- and pray to God those things should never happen again because what happened is unbelievable, and he can't imagine what was-- what was going on. This is my story, just-- just a bit of it. You've got a million of those stories. I don't know how many survived, but I'm sure everybody has a story. And we should pray this shouldn't happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think made you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Made me survive? To prove the world-- I wanted to prove there's-- I-- I [? wasn't ?] Jewish. And I wanted to prove that one Jew survived. I wanted to tell-- to-- to prove to tell them the story what happened because I-- in those years, I thought, nobody would know what was going on. You know? And-- and the second what I was fighting for, to-- for survival, is my family. I remember that I had some family left. And-- and they were-- they didn't know what happened to me, and I wanted to survive the war to be with my family to tell them what happened to the rest of the family, and that's the main reason I-- I was fighting for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we're going to see some pictures of the rest of the family now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK. Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looks great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you introduce your wife please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Molly, which I have known for the last, I would say, 49 years, I think, because I knew her before we got married. We're going to be married 47 years. And we have had a wonderful marriage, happy-- happy life, nice children, grandchildren. And let's hope we're going to live together as long as we can. Always together, not separate. I wouldn't like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, OK. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture which I picked up at a local photographer when I got back after the war. And that was taking in 1925. And at the top of the picture is my older sister, Ray. That's left to right. And the next one is my brother, Murray. And the next after him was my brother, [? Hymi. ?] And the next one is my brother, Meyer. On the second row on the bottom was my sister, Bayla. The next was my mother. And the middle of the picture is my older sister, Matla. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the middle-- in the bottom, that's me, in the middle between my father and my mother. That's me. Next is my father. And on his left, is my older-- youngest brother, [? Martin, ?] which he survived the war with me together. And there's one child missing in the picture. The one was 10 years old. She wasn't born yet. She was born in 1929. That's the story of our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Cmielow, my place of birth, you know. We were born in Cmielow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is-- on the left is my mother. And the next one is my sister, which was born in 1923. That picture was taken during the war before they took them away in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister? What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your sister's name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that-- my sister's name is Bayla, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bayla.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bayla.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year of that-- in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my youngest sister. She-- this was taken in 1939 just before the war, and she was 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Toba, which we gave her during the-- before-- before they took her away to the [? farm. ?] They brought her back to the same day when they took my parents, unfortunately. She would have been alive now. OK, this-- that-- that picture was taken in 1925. But in 19-- when they-- when they took out all the Jews, they'd round them up from several cities. And this square, that's what-- that was the last solution, like you say. They took from here to the trains, which was a couple miles away from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this area called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the area called? What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cmielow. My hometown, Cmielow, same-- same, Cmielow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my identification card. When I got to Germany, they gave me a passport, you know, from my birth papers. But this is-- which was the original change of name, but this is the passport, which the German gave me. You can see the-- the stamp there, my-- my photo. It's the [INAUDIBLE], they call it. You know. And that was taken in the beginning in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name-- my name is-- they had a Polish name-- is [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] that was the name I was carrying and this-- until the end of the war. All right, tell me when to talk. OK. In 1944, all of us on the picture, we worked in the same village. We decided to take a photo together. And on top of the first line on top right to left is a Frenchman, which he was a war prisoner. He worked in that village. And the next one is from Belgium, also a war prisoner, which worked in our village. And the rest of the line is all Russian, Ukrainian. And the second role, the four-- the-- sitting down, that's me. And next to me is another Polack, another Polish man next to me, the following. And the [INAUDIBLE], the rest are all Russian, which we worked together in that village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're the circled one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I point you out? I don't have to. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say, I'm the one circled. Oh, he said it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in 1944. Myself and my brother, [? Martin, ?] in the same village of, I think, where Martin worked. That's why we took that picture. I'm on the left side, and he is standing in the right side.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in 1938. And that-- on the picture, it was-- on the bottom of the picture, there's a bishop came to our hometown, which the custom was to greet him with the-- with the [? Sefer, ?] with the-- with the [? Sefer ?] and a bread and salt, you know, to-- to make peace amongst the Gentile and the Jews. That was the custom from the community. On the top of the picture from left to right, I forgot the bottom of the picture is my uncle holding the [? Sefer, ?] Uncle Akiva, the one I mentioned before in the interview. He's greeting the bishop. The upper of the picture is the first one with the beard was my father. And second one is the rabbi of the-- of the city. And the rest are members of the community, and that was the story about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is-- this is the The time when they brought Mr. Wenzel to Montreal, it was November, 1971, which all the newspapers came down to and other newspapers. They took the picture with the story about Mr. Wenzel, which stayed with us for six weeks at Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year? This year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken after his-- her bar mitzvah in '95. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture? Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you. This-- this is a picture taken at my oldest daughter-in-law's bat mitzvah-- [? MOLLY KOHN: ?] Granddaughter's. --granddaughter's bat mitzvah, 1995 in May some times. The first one on the picture from the left is Amanda, the one that the party was making for. And the second one sending is her mother Marlene, and the next one is her brother, Ryan. And after Ryan standing is-- on top of the picture is [? Nina, ?] Jeff's wife. And at the far end is Gina, is 12 years old. On the bottom sitting down is-- from left to right is my son, Jeff. And the little girl in between is Marley, and then the little boy is Joshua. And the last one is my oldest son, Stan. And that's the story of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Love the family.
